1: Eliphaz’s first speech is widely considered the theologically most sophisticated of the three friends’ contributions. His geographical origin — Teman — marks him as a representative of the Edomite wisdom tradition. Teman (modern Tawilan, near Petra, Jordan) was the capital region of Edom; Jeremiah 49:7 and Obadiah 1:8 both identify Teman with wisdom: “Is wisdom no more in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent?” The book of Job thus presents its most sophisticated theological opponent as the heir of the most renowned non-Israelite wisdom tradition.
2: Eliphaz begins with an elaborate apology for speaking, acknowledging the sensitivity of Job’s situation. In ancient wisdom discourse, this kind of hedge (“if one ventures a word with you, will it trouble you?”) was a standard rhetorical opener that demonstrated social awareness before delivering a potentially unwelcome message. Egyptian wisdom texts like the Instructions of Amenemope and Ptahhotep include similar hedging formulae before delivering hard wisdom. The aposiopesis (the self-interruption of the inner compulsion to speak overriding the social caution) was also a recognized rhetorical move.
3: Eliphaz’s acknowledgment of Job’s past role as a strengthener and teacher of others (yissarta rabbim, you have instructed many) is a recognition of Job’s social position as a wisdom teacher. In the ancient world, the sage who could provide counsel in distress was a valued social resource. Proverbs 31:26 describes the capable woman: “she opens her mouth in wisdom; the teaching of kindness is on her tongue.” Eliphaz is acknowledging that Job has played this exact role for others — which is precisely why his inability to apply his own counsel to himself is, in Eliphaz’s view, so striking.
4: The imagery of “stumbling ones” (mikhshol) being steadied and “buckling knees” (birkhayim kor’ot) being strengthened comes from the vocabulary of the wisdom teacher’s pastoral function. Isaiah 35:3 uses the same double image (“strengthen the weak hands and make firm the feeble knees”) in the context of eschatological encouragement. The pastoral function of wisdom — steadying the troubled, strengthening the despairing — was a recognized social role in the ancient world. Eliphaz is effectively saying: “you were a wisdom teacher; where is that wisdom now?”
5: The before-and-after contrast (’atah = now) is Eliphaz’s most pointed observation: Job counseled others effectively but cannot bear his own counsel when suffering touches him personally. The word translated “impatient” (tila’eh) comes from a root meaning to be weary, exhausted, worn out. This is not a moral condemnation but an observation about the gap between theoretical wisdom and embodied experience of suffering — a gap that remains a perpetual challenge in the wisdom traditions of every culture.
6: Eliphaz’s appeal to Job’s own piety as the source of his confidence (’yir’atekha kislat’kha, your fear of God is your confidence) is the friends’ most reasonable argument. In the Psalter, the God-fearer’s confidence is precisely that YHWH protects the righteous (Psalms 34:7; Job 91:1–4). The wisdom tradition consistently taught that the fear of YHWH was the foundation of security (Proverbs 14:26: “in the fear of YHWH one has strong confidence”). Eliphaz is not being gratuitously cruel; he is applying the received wisdom tradition to Job’s situation. The problem is that this tradition does not account for the book’s actual situation.
7: The retributive justice axiom — no innocent person perishes; no upright person is cut off — is the foundational moral claim of the friends and is deeply embedded in both OT Deuteronomic theology (Deut 28: blessings for obedience, curses for disobedience) and in ancient Near Eastern wisdom. The Babylonian Theodicy (ca. 1000 BCE) includes a parallel claim that suffering indicates sin; the Instructions of Amenemope and many Mesopotamian wisdom texts operate within the retributive-justice framework. The book of Job’s distinctive contribution to the wisdom tradition is its sustained challenge to this axiom through the case of undeniable innocent suffering.
8: The agricultural metaphor — plowing (harash) iniquity, sowing (zara’) trouble, reaping the same — reflects the agricultural world of the ancient Near East where sowing and reaping governed all understanding of causation. The metaphor appears throughout the OT (Hosea 8:7: “they sow the wind and reap the whirlwind”; Proverbs 22:8: “whoever sows injustice will reap calamity”; Galatians 6:7 in the NT). The agricultural law of causation (you reap what you sow) is universally intuitive and deeply embedded in all agricultural civilizations’ moral reasoning.
9: The breath of God (nismat ’el) and the blast of his nostrils (ruah ’apo) destroying the wicked reflects the storm-god imagery of the ancient Near East. In Ugaritic mythology, Baal’s power was expressed through storms and lightning; in Mesopotamian theology, Enlil’s breath (the wind) had cosmic destructive power. The OT consistently uses storm imagery for divine judgment (Psalms 18:15–16; Isaiah 30:28; Nahum 1:3). Eliphaz’s argument: the same divine breath that sustains the righteous (Genesis 2:7) destroys the wicked.
10: The lion imagery (v10–11) is among the most common metaphors in ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature for the powerful who are eventually brought low. Lions appear in Mesopotamian royal imagery as symbols of royal power (Assyrian kings hunted lions as a display of dominance); in Ugaritic texts, the lion is associated with the warrior-goddess Anat. In the OT, lions serve as metaphors for both the wicked powerful (Proverbs 28:15; Ezekiel 22:25) and for divine power (Isaiah 31:4; Hosea 5:14). Eliphaz’s use of the lion metaphor implies that even the most powerful wicked are ultimately destroyed.
11: The four lion-words in vv10–11 — ’aryeh (the generic lion), shahats (the fierce lion, perhaps a lioness), benei kefir (young lions), and layish (the mature/experienced lion) — reflect the precision of ancient vocabulary for a dangerous predator. The Ugaritic texts similarly distinguish multiple lion-types. The use of four lion-words in two verses is a display of the extensive vocabulary available to an educated ANE sage on the topic of predatory power and its limits.
12: Eliphaz’s claim of a nocturnal revelation raises the question of dream-incubation in the ancient world. In Mesopotamian practice, a person seeking divine communication could sleep in a temple (bitu) to receive a dream; in Egypt, the practice of incubation (sleeping at a sacred site to receive a divine dream) was widespread. The OT similarly recognizes nocturnal divine communication (Genesis 28:12 Jacob’s ladder; 1 Kgs 3:5 Solomon’s dream). Eliphaz’s revelation came unbidden, by stealth, as a faint whisper — which is consistent with the OT’s preference for non-dramatic divine communication (1 Kings 19:12: “after the fire, a sound of sheer silence”).
13: The deep sleep (tardema) in which divine revelation comes parallels the two other major uses of this word in the OT: Adam’s deep sleep in Genesis 2:21 (during which Eve is created) and Abraham’s in Genesis 15:12 (during which the covenant is enacted). In both cases, tardema precedes a divine act of creation or covenant. In the ancient world, the boundary between sleep and vision-trance was not sharply distinguished; both were recognized as states in which divine communication could occur. The Mesopotamian baru (diviner) literature discusses the various states of consciousness in which divine communication was received.
14: The physical effects of theophanic experience — fear, trembling, bone-shaking, bristling hair — are attested across the ancient world’s religious literature. In Mesopotamian texts, the experience of divine presence caused puluhtu (terror/awe) and physical symptoms including hair standing on end. The OT similarly describes the physical effects of divine encounter: Isaiah in the throne-room vision cries out “Woe is me!” (Isaiah 6:5); Daniel falls on his face (Daniel 10:9). The hair-bristling response (yisammer se’ar besari) is the same as what modern physiology calls piloerection (goosebumps) — the universal human response to intense fear or awe.
15: The spirit (ruah) passing before Eliphaz’s face uses a theophanic category familiar from the OT: the divine presence “passes before” as a manifestation of power (Exodus 33:19–22: YHWH’s glory passes before Moses; 1 Kings 19:11: “a great and strong wind” passes before Elijah). In the ancient world, wind/breath was understood as the most common form of invisible divine presence — the breath of the god that could be felt but not seen. Eliphaz’s description of a ruah passing his face, causing physical manifestations, places his experience in the category of genuine theophanic encounter.
16: The apparition’s standing still without visible form (temunah) followed by a voice distinguishes Eliphaz’s experience from Moses’s unique encounter at Sinai (Numbers 12:8: YHWH speaks to Moses “mouth to mouth” and Moses sees the temunah of YHWH). Deuteronomy 4:12 uses the same distinction to describe the Sinai encounter: “you heard the sound of words but saw no form (temunah).” Eliphaz received a voice-only revelation, without form — placed below Moses’s level but within the recognized range of genuine divine communication.
17: The rhetorical question — can a ’enosh (mortal human) be more righteous than God? — is the friends’ theological centerpiece. The Mesopotamian wisdom text Ludlul bel nemeqi includes the line: “Who knows the will of the gods in heaven? Who understands the plans of the underworld gods?” — emphasizing the incomprehensibility of divine decisions. The Egyptian Maxims of Ptahhotep counsel submission to the divine order as the foundation of wisdom. All ancient wisdom traditions share the baseline assumption that human beings cannot critique or surpass the gods; Eliphaz’s question is in this tradition.
18: The theological argument from divine standards — if God does not trust his servants or his angels, how much less can mortals be trusted — reflects a well-known argument pattern in ancient legal and wisdom thought (the qal vahomer, “light and heavy,” argument: if X is true in the less serious case, how much more in the more serious case). The concept of angels (mal’akhim) as divine servants who can make errors reflects an OT tradition in which the divine council members are fallible (Ps 82: God judges the “gods” who have failed in their judicial responsibilities).
19: The image of humans as batei homer (houses of clay) is closely paralleled in Mesopotamian creation texts. The Atrahasis epic describes human creation from clay mixed with divine blood; the Enuma Elish describes humans made from clay to do the work of the gods. The OT uses the same imagery: Genesis 2:7 (afar min-ha’adamah, dust from the ground), Isaiah 64:8 (we are the clay, YHWH is the potter), Psalms 103:14 (he knows that we are dust). Eliphaz draws on a cosmological assumption shared by the entire ancient world: humans are clay-creatures whose fundamental fragility is inscribed in their very constitution.
20: The observation that human life ends so quickly — “between morning and evening they are destroyed; without anyone noticing, they vanish forever” — reflects the ancient world’s acute consciousness of human transience. Psalms 90:3–6 (attributed to Moses) uses similar imagery: humans are swept away “like a dream, like grass that is renewed in the morning.” Egyptian funerary literature extensively meditates on the brevity of human life. The observation that individual human deaths go unnoticed by the cosmos (“without anyone noticing”) is a philosophical observation characteristic of the post-Bronze-Age wisdom traditions’ more naturalistic view of death.
21: The final image of the tent-peg (yeter) pulled up from within — leaving the tent without structural support, destined to collapse — uses a metaphor from the nomadic world of the ancient Near East where tents were the standard dwelling. The tent-peg that anchors the tent from the inside is the central structural element; when it is removed, the entire structure collapses. Paul uses the same tent metaphor in 2 Corinthians 5:1 (“if the earthly tent we live in is destroyed”). Eliphaz’s conclusion: mortals die before attaining wisdom (bemah hokhmah), making any human claim to righteousness before God epistemically groundless.
